---
title: "8.3 Matrix multiplication as a performance model"
---

## Explanation

Matrix multiplication is a useful example because it connects algorithmic
complexity, memory access, and optimized libraries.

If `A` has shape `N1 x N2` and `B` has shape `N2 x N3`, then `C = A * B` has
shape `N1 x N3`. The basic algorithm computes:

```julia
C[i, j] = sum(A[i, k] * B[k, j] for k in 1:N2)
```

The number of multiply-add terms is proportional to:

```text
N1 * N2 * N3
```

The input data size is proportional to:

```text
N1 * N2 + N2 * N3
```

Writing the output also costs `N1 * N3` elements. In real code, data may be
moved many more times if cache reuse is poor.

When `N1 * N2 * N3` is much larger than the input data size, matrix
multiplication can perform many arithmetic operations for each loaded value.
This can hide memory-bandwidth limits better than operations that read data once
and do very little arithmetic.

A simple Julia implementation is useful for learning:

```julia
function matmul_basic(A, B)
    n1, n2 = size(A)
    n2b, n3 = size(B)
    @assert n2 == n2b

    T = promote_type(eltype(A), eltype(B))
    C = zeros(T, n1, n3)

    for i in 1:n1
        for j in 1:n3
            s = zero(T)
            for k in 1:n2
                s += A[i, k] * B[k, j]
            end
            C[i, j] = s
        end
    end
    return C
end
```

For actual dense matrix multiplication, use Julia's built-in `A * B`. It calls
an optimized BLAS library, often OpenBLAS in a default Julia installation. The
optimized library uses cache-aware algorithms and low-level tuning.

For benchmarking, use `BenchmarkTools.jl`. If it is not installed, add it to
the Julia project environment first. Project environments are discussed later in
[Project structure, environment, and reproducibility](../reproducible-workflow/project-structure-and-reproducibility.qmd).

To compare single-thread performance, start Julia with one thread for OpenMP and
set BLAS threads to one:

```bash
OMP_NUM_THREADS=1 julia matrix_multiplication_benchmark.jl
```

```julia
using LinearAlgebra
using BenchmarkTools

BLAS.set_num_threads(1)

N1, N2, N3 = 128, 128, 128
A = rand(N1, N2)
B = rand(N2, N3)

@show BLAS.get_num_threads()
@show maximum(abs.(matmul_basic(A, B) .- A * B))

@btime matmul_basic($A, $B)
@btime $A * $B
```

## Things to look up

- Matrix multiplication
- Arithmetic intensity
- Memory bandwidth
- Cache blocking / tiling
- BenchmarkTools.jl
- BLAS
- OpenBLAS
- `BLAS.set_num_threads`

## Exercise

Write `matmul_basic(A, B)` and compare its speed with Julia's built-in `A * B`
using `BenchmarkTools.jl`. Use one BLAS thread. Then modify your implementation
to use blocking and try to improve its speed.

## Notes for the exercise

- First check correctness against `A * B`.
- Use matrices large enough that timing is meaningful.
- Record `N1`, `N2`, `N3`, BLAS thread count, and benchmark results.
- Do not optimize before checking correctness.
- Explain why blocking can reduce repeated memory transfer.
- Do not expect a simple hand-written implementation to beat optimized BLAS.
